text: The surgeon positions themselves on the right side of the patient as a thirty-degree laparoscope is inserted through the umbilical port, providing a clear view of the surgical field.
The surgeon uses a thirty-degree laparoscope to carefully observe the liver and explore the peritoneal surface, checking for any visible signs of tumor metastasis.
The surgeon locates the sigmoid tumor, visually identifying its position within the colon.
The surgeon identifies the proximal resection margin of the colon and marks it with a surgical clip, preparing for resection.
The surgeon retrieves free peritoneal fluid, if present, for cytological analysis using a laparoscopic approach.
The surgeon uses a transfixed stitch to suspend the uterus, enhancing exposure of the surgical field.
Several interesting anatomical details are seen in this case.
The surgeon identifies the left ureter, inferior mesenteric vein, left colic vein, left colic artery, and left kidney within the surgical field.
The surgeon identifies the left renal vein and the left gonadal vein. As the procedure progresses upwards, the inferior margin of the pancreas is visualized.
The surgeon follows the left colic vessels to reach the left colic flexure, revealing the anatomical junction between the transverse and descending colon.
The surgeon observes the gastric body through the transverse mesocolon, highlighting its anatomical position relative to the surrounding structures.
The surgeon locates the aortic plane and identifies the origin of the inferior mesenteric artery, visible in the surgical field.
The surgeon identifies several lymph nodes in the surgical field, likely located near the previously exposed left colic vessels and surrounding anatomical structures.
The inferior vena cava is visualized on the right side of the aorta.
The surgeon visualizes the promontorium while proceeding downwards in the surgical field.
The surgeon identifies important anatomical structures, including the inferior mesenteric artery and surrounding lymph nodes, in the surgical field.
The surgeon visualizes the mesosigmoid and the left ureter, highlighting their anatomical positions in relation to the surrounding structures.
The surgeon identifies the left gonadal vessels, which are visible in the surgical field.
The surgeon visualizes the common iliac artery, external iliac artery, hypogastric artery, right ureter, right gonadal vessels, and the pelvic inlet.
The right ureter is observed following the superior aspect of the hypogastric artery, highlighting its anatomical course in relation to the artery.
The right gonadal vessels are clearly visible crossing over the right external iliac vessels.

The surgeon clearly visualizes the right external iliac artery and vein, the right round ligament of the uterus, the right gonadal vessels, the right uterine tube, and the right ureter within the surgical field.
The right inguinal ligament is visible in the surgical field.
The surgeon identifies the path of the left common iliac vessels near the aortic bifurcation, with the vessels clearly visible in the surgical field.
The surgeon follows both ureters as they enter the pelvis, crossing the bifurcation of the common iliac vessels.
The surgeon highlights the close proximity of the left ureter to the proximal tract of the inferior mesenteric artery, emphasizing the anatomical relationship between these structures.
The surgeon identifies the intermesenteric plexus, which is visually distinct within the surgical field.
The surgeon begins the dissection by dividing the peritoneum between the inferior mesenteric vein and the aorta, avoiding direct pursuit of the inferior mesenteric artery.
The surgeon employs a strategy to visualize and divide the atolletes fasciae, effectively lowering the left ureter and moving it away from the inferior mesenteric artery.
The surgeon safely pursues the inferior mesenteric artery, ensuring it is separated from the left ureter and surrounding structures.
